content: We can frame zero-shot and few-shot prompting performance differences through an ICM curiosity lens: few-shot prompting acts like an intrinsic curiosity reward signal that guides a large language model to adjust its task search space toward novel but in-distribution solutions, just as ICM rewards autonomous agents for exploring under-visited state transitions. This leads to the testable hypothesis that prompting strategies that explicitly add 'novelty of observation' incentives aligned with ICM will improve few-shot generalization on out-of-distribution tasks far more than they improve zero-shot performance.
